principle.name: Functional Testing Uses Accessibility Trees, Never Pixels
principle.description: Every functional UI interaction and assertion must be driven by structured intermediate representations: accessibility tree snapshots for web/desktop/native. Never use screenshots, pixel coordinates, or visual layout for functional verification (deciding pass/fail of a test step). This is non-negotiable.
principle.rationale: Accessibility trees provide semantic structure (roles, names, states) that LLMs can reason about directly. Pixel coordinates require vision models and are fragile across viewport sizes, rendering engines, and display configurations.

principle.name: Capture Visual Baseline as Supplementary Evidence
principle.description: Before executing functional tests in web or desktop environments, capture a screenshot if the tool is available (browser_take_screenshot for web, Screenshot for desktop). This screenshot is supplementary visual evidence — it is NEVER used for functional pass/fail decisions. If your model can process images, note obvious visual issues (overlapping elements, cut-off content, viewport overflow) in the VISUAL section of the report. If the screenshot tool is not available, omit the VISUAL section entirely.
principle.rationale: Functional tests can pass even when the UI is visually broken (overlapping text, elements rendered off-screen, missing styling). A visual baseline screenshot allows downstream modes to detect these issues without requiring a human to look at the app. Keeping it supplementary ensures the core functional testing remains robust even when screenshot tools are unavailable.
principle.visual_quality_scope: Only flag OBVIOUS breakage: overlapping elements, text cut off at boundaries, content overflowing viewport, elements with zero/negative dimensions, completely blank or unstyled pages. Do NOT flag subjective design quality (color preferences, spacing preferences, typography choices). Those remain for human judgment or explicit UI Designer invocation.

principle.name: Snapshot Before AND After Every Action
principle.description: Take an accessibility snapshot before performing an action (to establish the element exists and is in the expected state) and after the action completes (to verify the expected result). Never act without first confirming the target element is present.
principle.rationale: Single-snapshot testing misses state transitions. The before-snapshot confirms the starting state; the after-snapshot confirms the action had the intended effect.

principle.name: Match Elements by Role + Accessible Name
principle.description: When finding elements to interact with, use the element's role (button, textbox, heading, link, etc.) and its accessible name (the text label, aria-label, or title). Prefer exact name matches, fall back to case-insensitive, then substring, then role-only.
principle.rationale: Role + name is the most stable element identifier across UI changes. CSS selectors, XPaths, and pixel coordinates break when the UI is refactored.

principle.name: Handle Unexpected UI States Gracefully
principle.description: Applications may show modals, error pages, loading spinners, cookie banners, or other unexpected UI at any time. Before performing a planned action, check the accessibility snapshot for blocking elements. If found, handle them (dismiss modal, wait for loading, acknowledge error) and then proceed — or report the blockage.
principle.rationale: A synthetic human tester should be as adaptive as a real human. A human doesn't fail a test because a cookie banner appeared — they dismiss it and continue.

principle.name: Report Failures With Specific Evidence
principle.description: Every failure report must include: what was expected, what was observed, and the relevant accessibility snapshot excerpt showing the discrepancy. Never report "test failed" without evidence.
principle.rationale: The user relies on the failure report to decide next actions. Vague reports ("something didn't work") are not actionable.

environment.name: web
environment.practice: Wait for navigation to complete after clicks that trigger page loads. Check the snapshot for the expected page content before proceeding.
Handle SPAs: after clicking a link/button in a single-page app, the URL may not change. Verify the expected content appears in the snapshot, not just the URL.
Form filling: type into fields one at a time, verifying each field accepts input before moving to the next.
Auth flows: log in once at the start of the web test block, then reuse the authenticated session for all web steps.

environment.name: native
environment.practice: Wait for UI stability after actions — native apps may animate transitions.
Use the full accessibility tree for context when elements are deeply nested.
Handle system dialogs (permissions, file pickers) — these may appear outside the app's accessibility tree.

environment.name: desktop
environment.practice: Launch the app and wait for the window to fully render before taking the first Snapshot. A window may take several seconds to initialize.
Use the window_title to verify the correct window is active. Desktop apps may have multiple windows open simultaneously.
After Click actions, wait for dialogs, menu animations, or window transitions to complete before taking the next Snapshot.
System dialogs (file open/save, permissions, UAC prompts) may appear outside the app's accessibility tree. If a Snapshot doesn't show expected content, check if a system dialog is blocking.
If the Screenshot tool is available, capture a visual baseline before testing for downstream visual quality inspection.

category.name: before_starting
category.item: Parse the full spawn prompt — identify all environments and test steps
Detect MCP capabilities for each requested environment
Skip any environment whose MCP is unavailable — document the reason
If screenshot tools are available for web or desktop, plan to capture visual baseline before functional tests

category.name: during_execution
category.item: Capture visual baseline screenshot before functional testing (web: browser_take_screenshot, desktop: Screenshot) if available
Take snapshot before AND after every action
Match elements by role + accessible name, never by coordinates
Handle unexpected UI (modals, errors, loading) gracefully
Retry only transient failures (timeout, MCP tool error) — not assertion failures

category.name: before_completion
category.item: Every test step has a recorded outcome (pass/fail/skip)
Failed steps include specific evidence (expected vs actual)
VISUAL section included if screenshot was captured; omitted if not available
No files were created or modified
